parameters.text:
=Role: Executive Assistant (Meeting Intelligence)
You are a highly precise Executive Assistant specializing in meeting intelligence and task extraction. Your sole focus is identifying actionable commitments made by Ryan Butler (rbutler@riverpointtechnology.com) and syncing them to Todoist.

Objective
Analyze the provided meeting transcript and context to isolate "to-dos" where Ryan is the explicit owner.

Instruction Pipeline
Speaker Identification: Map all mentions of "Ryan" or "rbutler" to the speaker list to ensure you are tracking the correct entity.

Task Extraction: Scan the transcript for verbs indicating commitment (e.g., "I will," "I'll take care of," "Assign that to me," "I'm going to").

Ownership Filter:

Include: Tasks explicitly assigned to Ryan or volunteered for by Ryan.

Exclude: Tasks assigned to others, general team brainstorming, or vague "we should" statements where Ryan is not the lead.

Deduplication: Review the context to ensure the task isn't a repeat of a previously discussed item already marked as "captured."

Execution: Use the Todoist MCP tool to create a task for every identified item.

Output & Tool Configuration
Todoist Project: Krisp

Priority/Due Date: Do not set (leave blank).

Task Title: Action-oriented and concise (e.g., "Email client regarding Q3 roadmap").

Task Description: Provide a 2-3 sentence summary of why this task was created based on the meeting context and include the specific quote from the transcript.

Strict Guardrails
Zero-Task Policy: If no specific, certain tasks for Ryan are found, your entire response MUST be: "No tasks identified for Ryan."

No Assumptions: If it is unclear who owns a task, do NOT assign it to Ryan.

No General Items: Do not create tasks for "Team" objectives unless the transcript explicitly states "Ryan, you are leading this."

Tool Usage: You MUST use the Todoist integration tool to create the tasks. If the tool is not used, you must provide a technical explanation as to why.

Input Data
<meeting_data>
Meeting Context: {{ $json.body.data.raw_meeting }}
Transcript: {{ $json.body.data.raw_content }}
</meeting_data>

IMPORTANT: If the tool is not used explain why

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: Nobrin, how are you?

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Hey, Jeff. Good.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: Hey, Jeff. Good. Was everything just live in the dream here?

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: All right, how are you doing?

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: We got one day it's 40, the next day it's 75. It's like it's April year.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Yeah, I was saying on one of the calls yesterday, we like, last week, we had nothing but tornado warnings, like, every single day, every single night.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: Yeah, I'm that crazy.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text:
We already broke the year record for the amount of tornadoes for the state just in one week's time.
So I think we're through it now. I think it's supposed to be seventy today. So hopefully in the right direction now.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: Yeah, but your... I mean, I assume Gus doesn't really have a lot of tornadoes. Do you like that? I mean, I know it's flat, but...

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text:
Yeah, here and there, but it's a rarity to have like back to back mornings. Like, we'll have them ran like probably one or two a year maybe, but having them over and over again like that's super rare. They're saying, like with El Nino and whatever the hell. Climate change, like the tornado alleys, like moving more north.
So we get to look forward to this.
So yeah.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: Yeah, that makes sense. Yeah, it was a weird winter, like, you know, co like kind of the mid Colorado, Utah. It didn't have a lot of snow, but we had like a horrible we had a horrible winter here.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: One, I'm curious about how the drought and fires are going to be out west this summer now with so little snow.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text:
It was one of the worst we've had since the seventies that I remember anyway, but yeah, I'd agree.
Well, cool. I don't know where. Dan, he's probably on a call, but just wanted to kind of how to up. And then we can. I don't think we're going to take a ton of time, but basically, effectively, what I'm trying to get to is, you know, I'm. I'm kind of talking to like some of the B of a folks and Trecar and everybody.
Like, you know, I think if we continue along with the path we're on, you know, it's gonna be unlikely we're going to continue there next year and longer just because of the way they're scrutinizing everything.
So there's really two things. One is what I'm trying to get at is like is there a way like with what we're doing, is there a way like we can be doing that on our side? And then poured it into their environment to get rid of the blockers.
And then. Secondly, you know, where can we potentially position like AI to them like they kind of. I want to go to, like, DG and just kind of be like we've kind of earned to right like, this is where you're. I'm just going to be like, Frank, like this is where you're gonna fail.
And here's why you're gonna fail. And like. And this is how we can help. You know, we really got nothing to lose at this point. So I'm just trying to get like, feedback from the crew to like, talking points to kind of make that play.
So it was really just that was really the kind of the goal of the discussion here. So I'm going to Dan tomorrow as well, so if he doesn't jump on, I could pick his brain down.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Yeah.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text:
But I just want to get your thoughts, kind of. Kevin and I discussed a little bit with Shricar last week.
And then I know, kind of from your perspective. Coming in not that long ago, I guess. But what you're seeing?

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text:
So it's funny because it's... I think kind of like what I'm seeing at the bank is the basis a lot for, like, the AI kind of like COE. You know, the band kind of flipped on AI, like Copilot and said, okay, guys, like, we're AI enabled.
And now everybody kind of go nuts with it, but there's been like, absolutely, like, no enablement kind of behind it. Like how to leverage it successfully. And I think there's a lot of room from that perspective, you know, even from like the terraformer side, you know, just kind of explaining a few of the basic concepts and then kind of harvesting, you know, what I've kind of taught them and then kind of like they're kind of taking that there.
So I think from, like, the advisory side, I think there's like a lot that can kind of be, I guess shown. The problem is it's just trying to get, you know, that audience, which is kind of where I'm kind of getting tied up on kind of the Terraformer side, but there's a lot there.
And I think kind of like the connectivity or like the connect to tissue. Kind of in between from like MCP development, you know, having AI talk to certain endpoints and certain technologies that they already have is possible as well because that's kind of like a black box for them right now as well.

content.speaker: Kevin Tuffner
content.text: Two.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text:
So yeah, the interesting point, Kevin, I think we talked about this like they have an initiative like they have to like they're supposed to like use AI to enhance and like quote unquote like save money.
But there's really nothing under like defining that is that correct?

content.speaker: Kevin Tuffner
content.text: Yeah, I think at the practitioner level, I think that's correct. I think at the leadership level, they have a different mandate, but it just hasn't trickled through.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Okay.

content.speaker: Kevin Tuffner
content.text: Like most works like Craig.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: It. Yeah, the other thing too, I'll say is it's interesting cause like, I don't really know who made the like the deciding factor of, like, to go with a GitHub Copilot, but like, one of the issues is like they're not really in a GitHub shop.

content.speaker: Kevin Tuffner
content.text:
Craig has it across his lieutenants like VG, Graham, and others.
It's just how do they distill that down through the work?

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: So a lot of, like the capability that Copilot offers, they're kind of like kneecaped, which I know it's like out of our like control, but just kind of wanted to mention it. So we're right.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: Just because I want their codes somewhere else or something like that.

content.speaker: Kevin Tuffner
content.text: They use Bitbucket like a whole other team owns the pipelines.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Right, yeah.

content.speaker: Kevin Tuffner
content.text: Yeah.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text:
So there's like a lot of capability of like GitHub, you know, maintaining the code within GitHub, but now that they don't have that, they lose a lot of that, which I don't know, but that's just kind of the bank.
But yeah, and I know KPMG is kind of in there, kind of sniffing around trying to identify use cases and things like that, but again, I think a big play is just like the enablement side.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: No, yeah, that's where I always thought because it seems like that's their biggest one of their biggest struggles is just enabling the consumers. It's like a car outside that has this glare on my window.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Yeah.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: So we're like, "It's blinding."

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: I see it on your face. Yeah.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text:
But one thing I when I was talking to Brian Haggerty, he brought up was like I think the one of the bigger, biggest challenges is like the dependencies around, like, you know, some of this.
And like, how do you keep track of that? Like is there are so many layers to this, right? Like that's if they make changes. What are the de what are the dependencies it's going to, like, fall prey to. Is that like a can of. Is that. Is that just like a bowl of spaghetti or is like. Is there a real, like, way to kind of like help untangle that for them, like, with what we're at least we're working on, like, if that makes sense.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Like, what kind of dependencies are you kind of thinking there? Or guess what was he kind of speaking to?

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: I mean, I think like just anything like kind of the you know, anything downstream like, you know, if they make a change number in their pipeline, like what does that do with within you know, there's so many teams, I guess, integrated into that stack like what's the like that I mean, I think it's complex.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Yeah.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: That's where I'm kind of struggling with it because I'm not technical, but like, I think the that's where I think they see some of the challenges as well with, like scaling. This, I guess, is one way to put it. But.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text:
I mean, I think from like the dependency issue is like that's already a problem at the bank. Making one change and impacting others.
And I think that's potentially where AI could help, you know, evaluating, you know, the code that's potentially going in and saying, hey, this is going to disrupt XYZ. You know, there's. I mean, it's kind of limitless on. On what we could potentially do here like approval workflows could adjust depending on, you know, what the code is actually doing or dependencies like you kind of name it.
It's we just kind of really need to, I think, kind of peel back the young in on some of these use cases. And I'm pretty positive we can help kind of develop whatever.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: Yeah, the that was kind of like Shricar Point was like, you know, okay, hey, it comes around like, okay, we spent $2 million with Riverpot.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: But again, it's getting that audience.

content.text:
Like, what did we get for it?
And like, you know, it's like, that's all I got for $2 million. And they understand, like, a lot of the challenges on their side, like, they know they're slow. They're, you know, they're a lot of blockers.
So, you know, he's the one that kind of suggested, like, you know, maybe it's like, hey, we'll do this in our environment, like we'll port it over like, you know, to get like, more done for same money, whatever.
But where I'm going with this, is that, like, realistic with what we're doing or is it like, do we really kind of need to be in their environment for, like, the activities we have going on?

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text:
I think it kind of depends a little bit on what kind of work stream we're kind of like working at. I think from say like the terraform side or the provider side, I think that is a lot more like code development.
I think that Workstream would we could probably be at the most value from like an AI perspective quickly. But then the other workstreams like I think we would have to kind of embed ourselves and get kind of an understanding of like use case and develop from that side. It would be a little different in kind of the patterns, but from like a value, I would say terraformer side.
And then kind of trickle that down into the other works. Work streams.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text:
Two. My daughter just got a kitten, and it's all over my desk right now. Yeah, I don't know. Kevin, what do you think in regards to that? Trying to find a different operating model with them that we can get more done than you... We're always blocked with their team and stuck somewhere, right?
Think, is that realistic with some of the stuff we've been scoped or they're looking to discuss or do?

content.text:
There is... I mean, I wrote some stuff down, and then I've got to clean it up for you, like we talked about yesterday. I mean, a lot of that is getting them to be honest. That's a come to Jesus conversation.
It's like you take that maturity framework I have. It's like I can do these five things in part two of the fly wheel. Like, can you knock down walls for me to do that? You know, like Ryan mentioned the Terra Farmers' code thing. We could totally do parts of that, like within our environment or use AI within their environment. They just can't get out of their own way. Both stubbornness, politics, you know, gaps within the environment, et cetera.
And so, like, you really need a leader to say, you know, cut the noise, pull the plug. Let's actually do this and bring results. Let's not talk about it, you know?

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: Yeah, I mean, isn't like, VG on the hook for a lot of this, like, in terms of like what he's committed to Craig, is it like.

content.speaker: Kevin Tuffner
content.text:
He's got the majority of the big rocks? I mean, Graham kind of owns the terraform kind of side of the house. I mean, I agree with some of the stuff he's doing and then some of the shit like. I don't know if I talk to you about it or Ian and I know I talked to Dan about it, but. Or Dan quack.
But, you know, like the whole building in AWS management console. Like that ship has sailed. Like, every bank has failed doing that. Like, just enable AI to do what you need. Like, I have an example on my list, which is the automation platform integrations with AI like IBM's got a huge gap they're trying to fill. Ryan of how do you get a do TF ansable Turbo all the shit to talk together like we can be that enabler or that USB cable, you know, to bring it together.
And so if they can just get out of their way and just like let us spin this up and bring that value in that incubate, you know, kind of area. You know, there's some value there, but when we continue to do dumb things like cut costs or not cut costs, but, cut corners on, you know, features and tech like, I mean, none of the shits ever going to happen.
Like, I mean, I'm mainly blaming Graham's a or because his or is just stubborn. He's. I think Danny, I think is probably gonna make changes in a few months because he's noticed it and he's got huge gaps in PMO he's got huge gaps in our bonus team.
So I think that will just iron its own self out just due to stupidity on, you know, his direct reports. But like the VG side, it's just like, you know, everything that he's got, it's like, let's chisel away at some of the shit for you. Can you sponsor it?
You know, like, if you can't sponsor it, you know, then it's never going to happen. You know, like, let us fail. You know, we're kind of putting our cards on the table. Yeah, and it could be anything. It could be like I have like AI migration factory of OPENSHIFT to Tonzuo. Someone has to fucking do that.
Like you got to convert all those containers, you've got to move manifest, you've got to move to storage volumes. Like a I should be doing that, not paying KPMG $10 million to do it. You know, you had the IBM thing, which I think we're going to do it anyway just because IBm sees they have a gap here and their portfolio is not there yet.
Like, we'll kind of beat them to some of that stuff. There's things like skills copy, there's unit testing, QA testing. You know, why does this shit take 42 people in India? You know, there's a blade logic to answerable workstream that needs to be done like refactor that code and you know, in kill blade logic like these are, you know, tech that things that just need to come off the table. Why not use AI?

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: So the conversation that I mentioned like that with DG is that same thing. Is that same conversation applicable to Graham, or do we just not want to really engage with Graham's team because they're just...?

content.speaker: Kevin Tuffner
content.text:
I'd roll at V GI first. See what he does. Because he owns the majority of the biger rocks. Graham is kind of stubborn, and I bet he'll be open to it, but like, he kind of already made his mind, like, he because I challenged him on a graph database. I'm like, you know, why don't we just enable Infograph for you instead of you build this like how? She's like, I get it's in SaaSs, but like, we could figure out an exception and bring it on. Prem you guys are big enough.
Like, if IBM can't do that, we have bigger challenges. But, like, he's kind of got his mind made up of, like, this portal that does, like, cost control and all this shit like AWUS. And like, I, you know, I just sit there and Jim Ray and I joke were like JP's failed cities. Failed.
Like what makes you able to carry the torture, you know? And so I feel like we just go after V GI get a couple wins because he owns more of the real estate. I think we can impact from an AI like, if you look at Graham, it's just really terraform code and some API shit at the moment, you know?
And then I kind of want to stick a fork in his eye on the whole service now. Shit like, you know, we're doing all this automation, but then you guys are going back to click ops, like, why not? Someone logs in the copilot and say, give me a VM us this template and it goes and fucking does it.
Like, who wants to go through a website in 2026, right? You know?

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: Well, it seems like Veg's, or at least Ryan and Kakar and Trick, they're at least more open to these kind of conversations. Yeah, you know, I think Graham's to your point is kind of made, you know, he is the smartest guy.

content.speaker: Kevin Tuffner
content.text: Put his cards on the table already. And I think he's got to deliver for Craig.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text:
But, I mean, he's just, you know, to your point, he's just he's in the. He's been yaking for three years. I've known him. He knows what he wants and like, yeah, I not really gonna change that, I guess, but, yeah, I don't know, son, what would be helpful and kind of what I asked Kevin is like, if you could just send me like, a few bullet points around, like things that and I can. I want and I need to put that together to kind of get time with. I'm trying to get in person time with them in Jersey to just like, hey, these are the things we think we can impact. This is what we want to talk about. Come in and kind of like, demonstrate, you know, go through it with you.
So I'm just trying to get, like, a few talking points. So if you could like summarize like a few key points from your side like AI and or like kind of doing things like we just take it off the plate into our environment like how we could really have some more meaningful impact.
Like what I what I'm trying to get in front of is just again, we got to like re restructure the way we're engaged with them for a for more value, but B like they're just in this mode of like AI's solving all problems.
Like we rolled out coput so we can eliminate, you know, 20% of our developers and all of our SW contractors. And reality, you're not like that's just kind of what the mentality is.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Yeah.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text:
So I'm trying to take a different approach.
I think we've earned a seat at the table to just be direct, like we've got nothing to lose at this point. Just be like this and this is fucked up and you're going to fail if not. But we least got to go on we at least got to go on record with it like.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Yeah.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: And I'm happy to have that conversation. I think we need to at this point, we're running out of runway with them. But for the year, the...

content.speaker: Kevin Tuffner
content.text: 27th to TBD. I don't think we're going to get half of what it is.

content.text:
And it's not going to be the same. And I mean, who knows where the programs even go, right? Like things to be changing so quickly, like if they even, you know, they could change their rocks completely, who knows.
Like, you know how that happens every six months, it changes. So anyway, I'm trying so I'm kind of gated with Ryan Haggerty and Canacar shut his name I always fu up his name.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text:
He's like new. He came in from JP so him and Ryan Hag, he split roles.
So Ryan's really more like strategy kind of. And then Kindcor is more like foundation platform I think is kind of. So Shricar reports in the Cannacarno, they both report into VG so I'm trying to get on VG's calendar with get done the sponsor a meeting and they've been saying they would they're just trying to like this is what we want to I want to be like very specific, like this is the agenda. These are the talking points, and I want to make them like, you know, something compelling to meet with us. Not just like meat the meat kind of thing. I don't. I'm not going to try to waste our time without that.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Yeah. Okay, yeah, because I know talking to, like, Dan Kelly, it seems like Shricar is more of like a champion of, like the AI strategy or just doing stuff with AI versus what we're kind of seeing on like the.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text:
So anything you could shoot over to me would be great. And Kevin will send me and I'll ping. I'm hooking up with Dan tomorrow. I can get something from him. And then from there, I think I can put a story together.
Well, he's reading the tea leaves. He's like, every year they're like, cut for developers and cut, you know, he's like, if I put your SW into, like, chat PT, he's like, you know, I may not even need it, right?

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Yeah.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text:
Like, he's like, I don't know what I meant by that, but he's like, yeah, basically, he's the one that's just like, which I appreciate. He's actually being like, he's kind of coaching like, hey, like, we gotta try and do something. You gotta differentiate. You gotta do something different.
Because. Like. To your point, like KPM G's in there and they probably who knows what kind of smoke they're blowing up. The as that they're never going to deliver on, but that could like sabotage the things that we're working on as well.
So I mean, and I know IBM's in there like kind of saying like, we'll stitch all this together for you, anyone. We know they can't, right? There's like, you know, we just it's got to be proactive with them.
So he's been more of a coach on it and which is helpful. And he built some stuff in his own environment. He showed us around leveraging AI that's like. So, like. Yeah, to your point, like, his mind's definitely there.
And like, you know, and he's saying his guidance is they're starting to become a little more liberal with AI in the sense of like, getting value from it. Like it was so restrictive before now they're starting to be like there's much more openness to look at things, I guess, than there was previously.
Yeah, like it was very restrictive and that, you know, but they're, you know, I think they're to your point, like, you know, one of the. I know one of the mandates last year was like, hey, we got to cut a billion dollars and get 40% efficiency across rolls, and it's like, well, okay, great. How do you quantify that? What does that even mean?
Like, you know, to your point, like. Like, you know, where's that kind of that whole intake workflow? Like, just dumping a toll out in the into the wild isn't really going to check that kind of.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Right.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text:
Or even if it does, like, how do you quantify that it did do that?
Like, what does that even mean? Like could be cutting 40% of your staff are you like getting 40% more value like what do you how do you quantify what that even means to you? But so. But I mean, and you know, we're on the right initiatives. I just think we've just got to kind of be more proactive and direct with them.
And like we I think we've already a seat at the table to challenge them a little more. I'm not saying we haven't, but I mean, at a leadership level, like BG and them, like, we just really haven't taken that to them to this point.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Yeah. Okay, sounds good.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text:
Cool. Anything you guys had, that was all I really had.
If you could just guys both shoot me that, you know, today or tomorrow, it'd be awesome.

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: Yeah, that's really all I got.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text:
All right, toy you good very guys have a good what is today Tuesday?
I was saying it was Thursday when I woke up today, so I'm all fucked up, like, I thought I heard the garbage trucks. I'm like, is it garbage day? [Laughter] Yeah, it's like, what's your garbage truck here?

content.speaker: Ryan Butler
content.text: That sinking feeling. No. [Laughter] Yeah.

content.speaker: Kevin Tuffner
content.text: You got to wait to hold another week.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: It's about like, wrong day of the week TOS.

content.speaker: Kevin Tuffner
content.text: Or like last week they didn't pick up my grass bin because what happened? And I was like, Wow, I can't really cut grass this weekend. It's already full.

content.speaker: Jeff Eiben
content.text: Yeah, be overflowed. So all right guys, have a good one.

content.speaker: Kevin Tuffner
content.text: Thanks, guys. Later.

options.systemMessage:
Role: You are a highly precise Executive Assistant specializing in meeting intelligence and task extraction. Your sole focus is identifying actionable commitments made by Ryan (rbutler@riverpointtechnology.com) and preparing them for Todoist integration.

Objective:
Analyze the transcript and context to isolate "to-dos" where Ryan is the explicit owner.

Step-by-Step Instruction Pipeline:
Speaker Identification: Map all mentions of "Ryan" or "rbutler" to the speaker list to ensure you are tracking the correct entity.

Task Extraction: Scan the transcript for verbs indicating commitment (e.g., "I will," "I'll take care of," "Assign that to me," "I'm going to").

Ownership Filter: * Include: Tasks explicitly assigned to Ryan or volunteered for by Ryan.

Exclude: Tasks assigned to others, general team brainstorming, or vague "we should" statements where Ryan is not the lead.

Deduplication: Review the context to ensure the task isn't a repeat of a previously discussed item already marked as "captured."

Data Structuring: Format the output strictly as a JSON object (or the format required by your automation tool) for Todoist.

Output Formatting Rules:
Project: Always set to Krisp.

Priority/Due Date: Leave blank/Do not include.

Task Title: Action-oriented and concise (e.g., "Email client regarding Q3 roadmap").

Description: Provide a 2-3 sentence summary of why this task was created based on the meeting context and include the specific quote from the transcript.

Strict Guardrails:
Zero-Task Policy: If no specific, certain tasks for Ryan are found, your entire response MUST be: "No tasks identified for Ryan."

No Assumptions: If it is unclear who owns a task, do NOT assign it to Ryan.

No General Items: Do not create tasks for "Team" objectives unless the transcript explicitly states "Ryan, you are leading this."
